narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 34, your academic profile shows solid but not standout performance compared to peers who typically succeed in law school and on the bar exam. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 62% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger odds of bar passage to maintain their success rates. The key factors driving this prediction were your class rankings from earlier and later in your undergrad years, along with your family income level. Most influential was your third-year decile ranking of 3, which placed you in the lower third of your class—well below the average of around the middle of the pack—and this strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours correlate with tougher outcomes on the exam. Next in importance was your upper-middle family income, which unexpectedly nudged the prediction further toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns where students from similar backgrounds faced other hurdles in high-stakes legal prep. Your first-year decile of 3, also below the typical mid-range average, added to this risk by reinforcing a pattern of underwhelming early performance relative to peers. While your LSAT and GPA were close to averages (36.7 and 3.21, respectively), they weren't enough to offset these top concerns. In short, compared to typical admitted students who rank higher in their classes and show profiles that predict bar passage over 50% of the time, your combination signaled higher risk to the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts—but the automated system is conservative. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your class rank through grad school or additional coursework if possible, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience and bar prep readiness. Reapplying next cycle with these improvements could flip the model's prediction in your favor, opening doors to law school.
